title: An Analytical Model With a Generalized Nonlinear Water Transfer Term for the Flow in Dual-Porosity Media Induced by Constant-Rate Pumping in a Leaky Fractured Aquifer

research.question: Can a weighted nonlinear matrix-to-fracture transfer term reproduce early, intermediate, and late pumping drawdown in leaky dual-porosity aquifers better than fixed first- or second-order transfer terms?
research.system: An infinite radial fractured aquifer represented by connected fracture and lumped matrix continua, overlain by a leaking aquitard and pumped at constant rate by a fully penetrating well with wellbore storage.
research.inputData: Fracture and matrix hydraulic and storage properties, transfer coefficient, leakage, pumping geometry, and drawdown records from Whiteshell Laboratories, Canada, and Maheshwaram Watershed, India.
research.method: Introduce a weighting factor into a generalized nonlinear transfer term, derive Laplace-domain fracture and matrix drawdown with successive integration by parts, invert with CME, verify by finite elements, analyze sensitivity, and fit competing models to two field tests.
research.benchmark: First-order, second-order, and distributed-parameter dual-porosity solutions; a Kelvin-transformed finite-element solution; and field drawdown judged with SEE, ME, AIC, and BIC.
research.uncertainty: Normalized sensitivities identify when parameters are estimable; the leakage parameter is weakly sensitive. Information criteria penalize the extra weight parameter, but no confidence intervals are reported.
research.conclusion: For the two tested pumping records, fitted interior weights of 0.31 and 0.77 improve the reported fit and information criteria relative to the fixed transfer endpoints.

narrative.throughline: Water released from matrix blocks into fast fractures needs a continuously adjustable transfer law, not a forced choice between two endpoints.
narrative.audienceStartingPoint: The audience understands that fractures transmit water rapidly while rock matrix stores and releases it slowly.
narrative.stakes: A wrong exchange law distorts the S-shaped drawdown curve and biases storage, transfer, and leakage estimates from pumping tests.
narrative.resolution: A fitted weight moves between first- and second-order exchange and returns to two measured drawdown records with better penalized fit.

background.premise: Dual-porosity models treat fractures and matrix blocks as separate but communicating pore systems.
background.visibleEvidence: A pumped fracture network drains rapidly while matrix blocks feed water laterally into it.
background.whyNow: The two communicating storage domains are the physical object behind the transfer term.
background.boundary: The matrix is spatially lumped in the proposed model rather than explicitly discretized by shape.
background.sourceExcerpt: a fractured aquifer, which is characterized as two separate but interconnected pore
background.visualObject: Fast fracture channel embedded in water-storing matrix blocks
background.motionPurpose: Separate rapid fracture drainage from delayed matrix release.
background.stepDetail: Start pumping in the fracture, lower fracture head first, and show matrix water crossing the interface afterward.
background.transitionBridge: The delayed exchange becomes the unknown source term Gamma.

background.premise: Fixed first- and second-order transfer laws reproduce different parts of the pumping response but each misses part of the observed curve.
background.visibleEvidence: FOT, SOT, and distributed-parameter drawdown curves diverge across early, intermediate, and late time windows.
background.whyNow: This establishes why an adjustable transfer law is needed.
background.boundary: The comparison concerns the selected lumped and distributed dual-porosity formulations under pumping.
background.sourceExcerpt: the drawdown curve predicted by the LPA-based model with the FOT exhibits
background.visualObject: Three drawdown curves crossing time-regime bands
background.motionPurpose: Highlight that no endpoint curve remains closest in every time regime.
background.stepDetail: Reveal early, intermediate, and late bands, trace each benchmark curve, and mark its characteristic mismatch.
background.transitionBridge: The two endpoint laws become the endpoints of one weight slider.

background.premise: Aquitard leakage supplies an additional water source to the fracture continuum.
background.visibleEvidence: A vertical leakage arrow enters the fractured aquifer separately from matrix exchange.
background.whyNow: The field setting requires separating interporosity transfer from leakage.
background.boundary: Leakage is represented by a lumped linear term rather than explicit aquitard storage dynamics.
background.sourceExcerpt: model also accounts for the leakage from an overlying or underlying aquitard
background.visualObject: Overlying aquitard feeding the pumped fracture layer
background.motionPurpose: Add a distinct vertical source path without confusing it with matrix release.
background.stepDetail: Retain matrix arrows, introduce the aquitard above, and add a distinct vertical leakage path.
background.transitionBridge: Both source terms enter the coupled radial balance.

methods.input: Radial fracture flow with storage, pumping, matrix exchange, and aquitard leakage
methods.output: Fracture and lumped-matrix drawdown equations linked by Gamma
methods.validity: Fracture permeability dominates matrix permeability, supporting a spatial fracture PDE and time-only matrix storage equation.
methods.sourceExcerpt: flow equation for the matrix block by neglecting the matrix permeability can
methods.visualObject: Radial fracture PDE coupled to a lumped matrix storage box
methods.motionPurpose: Route the same transfer arrow out of matrix storage and into fracture flow.
methods.stepDetail: Balance radial fracture flux and storage, subtract leakage and Gamma, then place Gamma with opposite sign in the matrix storage balance.
methods.transitionBridge: The undefined Gamma is replaced by a weighted nonlinear law.

methods.input: Fracture drawdown sf and matrix drawdown sm
methods.output: A generalized transfer term Gamma controlled by weight omega
methods.validity: The expression exactly reduces to FOT at omega = 0 and SOT at omega = 1.
methods.sourceExcerpt: weight and its value ranges from zero to one. Equation 5 reduces
methods.visualObject: Weight slider morphing the transfer arrow between FOT and SOT endpoints
methods.motionPurpose: Make the generalized law visibly contain both legacy laws.
methods.stepDetail: Place FOT and SOT at slider endpoints, move omega through the interval, and continuously reshape the matrix-to-fracture flux.
methods.transitionBridge: The nonlinear weighted balances are nondimensionalized for solution.

methods.input: The dimensional nonlinear dual-porosity initial-boundary problem
methods.output: Scaled equations containing SD, CD, gamma_D, sigma_D, and KD
methods.validity: The chosen scales use well radius, fracture diffusivity, pumping rate, and fracture storage to preserve the governing ratios.
methods.sourceExcerpt: 9, Equation 1 with Equation 5 can be expressed in dimensionless form
methods.visualObject: Physical well-and-aquifer geometry collapsing onto dimensionless radial and time axes
methods.motionPurpose: Replace units with governing ratios before transformation.
methods.stepDetail: Scale drawdown by Q/(4 pi Kf b), radius by rw, time by Ssf rw^2/Kf, and group leakage, transfer, storage, and permeability ratios.
methods.transitionBridge: The compact nonlinear equations are ready for the specialized Laplace treatment.

methods.input: Dimensionless nonlinear fracture and matrix equations
methods.output: Laplace identities for nonlinear powers and derivative products
methods.validity: Successive integration by parts supplies Laplace identities for powers and products that ordinary linear transform rules cannot handle directly.
methods.sourceExcerpt: introduce a successive integration by parts formula into the Laplace transform method.
methods.visualObject: Nonlinear power and derivative-product terms unfolding into Laplace identities
methods.motionPurpose: Make the specialized transform rule available without yet solving the coupled system.
methods.stepDetail: Apply successive integration by parts to the nonlinear power and product terms and retain their Laplace-domain identities.
methods.transitionBridge: The transformed matrix equation is isolated in M05.

methods.output: The algebraic relation between matrix and fracture drawdown smD equals psi sfD
methods.validity: The positive-drawdown branch is selected for the pumping-induced dual-porosity response.
methods.sourceExcerpt: Equation A1 can be solved as
methods.visualObject: Transformed matrix balance collapsing into the multiplier psi
methods.motionPurpose: Replace the matrix unknown with one algebraic multiple of fracture drawdown.
methods.stepDetail: Select the positive-drawdown branch and write the matrix response as psi times the fracture response.
methods.transitionBridge: The proportionality is inserted into the fracture equation in M06.

methods.input: Transformed fracture equation and matrix relation smD equals psi sfD
methods.output: A radial fracture equation containing only transformed fracture drawdown
methods.validity: Equation A4 removes the matrix unknown while preserving the generalized transfer coefficient in the radial balance.
methods.sourceExcerpt: Substituting the result in Equation A4 into Equation A5, the flow equation for the fracture drawdown can
methods.visualObject: Matrix-response box collapsing into the radial fracture equation
methods.motionPurpose: Reduce the coupled transformed equations to one radial unknown.
methods.stepDetail: Replace smD with psi sfD and collect the remaining transfer terms around fracture drawdown.
methods.transitionBridge: The reduced radial equation admits the Bessel solution in M07.

methods.input: Reduced radial fracture equation with transformed well and far-field boundaries
methods.output: Laplace-domain fracture and matrix drawdown in Equations 17 and 18
methods.validity: Remote decay removes the growing modified-Bessel branch, and the well boundary determines the remaining coefficient.
methods.locator: PDF pages 15-16, Appendix A, Equations (A7)-(A11); PDF page 6, Equations (17)-(21)
methods.sourceExcerpt: The general solution of Equation A6 can be expressed in terms of the combination of two modified Bessel
methods.visualObject: Growing and decaying Bessel branches with only the remote-decaying branch retained
methods.motionPurpose: Apply the radial boundaries to obtain the transformed drawdown fields.
methods.stepDetail: Display both Bessel branches, remove the growing branch at infinity, determine the well coefficient, and recover matrix drawdown from psi.
methods.transitionBridge: The solved transformed fields are inverted to pumping time in M08.

methods.output: Time-domain dual-porosity drawdown and transfer flux
methods.validity: The concentrated matrix-exponential inversion is selected for monotonicity preservation and numerical stability.
methods.sourceExcerpt: Gibbs oscillation, preserving the monotonicity of the function, and being numerical stable
methods.visualObject: Laplace-domain Bessel expression unfolding into S-shaped drawdown curves
methods.motionPurpose: Return the analytical solution to the pumping-test time axis without oscillatory artifacts.
methods.stepDetail: Evaluate the CME nodes, sum the inversion weights, and reveal sf, sm, and Gamma through time.
methods.transitionBridge: The recovered curves are independently checked by finite elements.

methods.input: The infinite-domain nonlinear initial-boundary problem and analytical curves
methods.output: A finite radial coordinate and Kelvin-transformed fracture equation
methods.validity: A Kelvin transform maps the infinite radius to a finite mesh while preserving the transformed boundary conditions.
methods.sourceExcerpt: the Kelvin transformation (Ventura et al., 2018) is employed to map the radial coordinate from
methods.visualObject: Infinite radial axis folding into the finite interval from zero to one
methods.motionPurpose: Create a finite computational domain for the verification model.
methods.stepDetail: Replace rD by its reciprocal coordinate and carry the fracture equation onto the finite interval.
methods.transitionBridge: The finite coordinate supports the mesh solution in M10.

methods.input: Kelvin-transformed fracture equation and unchanged matrix equation
methods.output: Finite-element fracture drawdown over the finite radial mesh
methods.validity: The model uses the transformed boundary conditions and the reported maximum mesh size of 10^-4 in rD prime.
methods.sourceExcerpt: the finite-element model consisting of the above equations is performed with the maximum mesh
methods.visualObject: Finite radial interval filled with the reported verification mesh
methods.motionPurpose: Generate the numerical fracture-drawdown response on the mapped domain.
methods.stepDetail: Apply the transformed boundaries, discretize the interval, and solve the nonlinear finite-element equations.
methods.transitionBridge: The numerical response is sampled at the reported radii in M11.

methods.input: Analytical and finite-element fracture drawdown fields
methods.output: Paired temporal drawdown curves at 1, 5, and 10 m
methods.validity: The paper evaluates both solutions at the same three physical radii over the same time range.
methods.sourceExcerpt: present solution and finite-element solution observed at r = 1, 5, and 10 m for t from
methods.visualObject: Three radial observation markers feeding paired drawdown curves
methods.motionPurpose: Place both model outputs on identical distance-specific time axes.
methods.stepDetail: Mark the three radii and transfer each analytical and numerical time series to its own aligned axis.
methods.transitionBridge: The paired samples are compared directly in M12.

methods.input: Paired analytical and finite-element drawdown curves at three radii
methods.output: Verification of the analytical derivation across the sampled distances
methods.validity: Agreement is assessed over 1 second to 1.16 days for the paper's default parameter set.
methods.sourceExcerpt: drawdown curves predicted by both solutions are in good agreement
methods.visualObject: Analytical and finite-element curves overlaid at each sampled radius
methods.motionPurpose: Make any distance-specific disagreement visible before inference.
methods.stepDetail: Overlay each paired curve and inspect their separation across the reported time interval.
methods.transitionBridge: Verified curves are used for the explicit weight-response experiment in M13.

methods.input: Dual-porosity response with weight omega varied from zero to one
methods.output: Weight-dependent matrix-to-fracture flux and fracture and matrix drawdown curves
methods.validity: Figure 5 varies omega in increments of 0.25 at a fixed observation radius and reports convergence of drawdown curves at late time.
methods.locator: PDF pages 8-9, Section 3.1 Effect of Weight Value on DP Flow, Figure 5
methods.sourceExcerpt: fracture drawdown decreases with increasing  and the matrix drawdown increases with 
methods.visualObject: Omega-indexed transfer-flux and drawdown curve families across time
methods.motionPurpose: Show how the generalized weight changes early and intermediate dual-porosity response.
methods.stepDetail: Sweep omega from zero to one, trace the flux and two drawdown families, and retain their reported late-time convergence.
methods.transitionBridge: The response experiment motivates parameter sensitivity calculation in M14.

methods.input: Fracture drawdown and each aquifer parameter
methods.output: Time-resolved sensitivity curves for Kf, Ssf, Ssm, sigma Km, gamma, and omega
methods.validity: A 10^-3 forward perturbation approximates the normalized derivative used for identifiability screening.
methods.sourceExcerpt: is the most sensitive parameter to sf and its normalized sensitivity value
methods.visualObject: Sensitivity curves occupying early, intermediate, and late time bands
methods.motionPurpose: Associate fracture storage, transfer, matrix storage, and leakage with distinct observation windows.
methods.stepDetail: Perturb one parameter at a time, normalize the drawdown change, and align each sensitivity peak with its governing time regime.
methods.transitionBridge: The sensitivity windows guide fitting of the two field records.
methods.minimalText: Each parameter has a time window

methods.input: Whiteshell W4 drawdown observations and the three competing transfer models
methods.output: Three fitted parameter sets including generalized-model omega equal to 0.31
methods.validity: The leakage term is retained for this site because the source reports leakage from the overlying clay-loam till.
methods.sourceExcerpt: The results of parameter estima- tion determined by the present solution, FOT solution, SOT solution
methods.visualObject: Whiteshell measured drawdown with generalized, FOT, and SOT fitted curves
methods.motionPurpose: Estimate each transfer model's parameters from the same Whiteshell record.
methods.stepDetail: Fit each model to W4 drawdown, overlay its curve, and place the generalized weight at 0.31.
methods.transitionBridge: The three fitted models are ranked with penalized criteria in M16.

methods.input: Whiteshell fitted residuals and model parameter counts
methods.output: The generalized model's lower reported SEE, ME, AIC, and BIC values
methods.validity: AIC and BIC assess goodness of fit with penalties for model complexity; the comparison is limited to the three fitted transfer models and this record.
methods.sourceExcerpt: AIC, and BIC values for the present solution are all smaller than
methods.visualObject: Whiteshell scalar SEE, ME, AIC, and BIC rows for the three models
methods.motionPurpose: Separate model selection from parameter fitting and show the reported complexity penalty.
methods.stepDetail: Align the four reported criteria by model and mark the smallest value in each row.
methods.transitionBridge: A second record is fitted independently in M17.

methods.input: Maheshwaram IFP-16 drawdown observations and the three competing transfer models
methods.output: Three fitted parameter sets including generalized-model omega equal to 0.77
methods.validity: Leakage is omitted during parameter estimation because the source states that this aquifer is not under leaky conditions.
methods.sourceExcerpt: present solution, FOT solution, and SOT solution are used to analyze the drawdown data collected from well IFP-16.
methods.visualObject: Maheshwaram measured drawdown with the weight slider settled near SOT
methods.motionPurpose: Estimate each transfer model's parameters from the same IFP-16 record.
methods.stepDetail: Remove leakage, fit each model to IFP-16 drawdown, overlay its curve, and place the generalized weight at 0.77.
methods.transitionBridge: The three Maheshwaram fits are ranked in M18.

methods.input: Maheshwaram fitted residuals and model parameter counts
methods.output: The generalized model's smallest reported SEE, ME, AIC, and BIC values
methods.validity: The comparison is limited to the three fitted transfer models and the IFP-16 record, with no confidence intervals reported.
methods.sourceExcerpt: Table 2 shows that the values of SEE, ME, AIC, and BIC for the present solution are all the smallest.
methods.visualObject: Maheshwaram scalar SEE, ME, AIC, and BIC rows for the three models
methods.motionPurpose: Keep the second model selection attached to its own field record.
methods.stepDetail: Align the four reported criteria by model and mark the smallest value in each row.
methods.transitionBridge: The two record-specific fitted weights return to the physical transfer object.

symbols.visualObject: Drawdown in the connected fracture network
symbols.transition: Attach sf to the fast fracture-head decline.
Attach sm to the delayed matrix-head decline.

symbols.visualObject: Water-transfer arrow from matrix to fracture
symbols.transition: Transform the shared exchange arrow into Gamma.

symbols.meaning: Weighting factor in the generalized transfer term
symbols.transition: Place omega on the transfer-law slider.
Collapse aquitard conductivity and thickness into gamma.

return.visualObject: The pumped fracture network beside two separately labeled fitted weights for the Whiteshell and Maheshwaram records
return.claim: For the two tested pumping records, the generalized model fits empirical interior weights of 0.31 and 0.77 and has lower reported fit and information-criterion values than the fixed endpoints.
return.claimBoundary: The two weights are record-specific empirical estimates under the lumped-matrix radial model, not a general law that real matrix-fracture exchange must lie between the endpoints. Weak leakage sensitivity and absent confidence intervals constrain parameter certainty.
return.sourceExcerpt: Whiteshell Laboratories and Maheshwaram Watershed are respectively 0.31 and 0.77, indicating that

visual.primaryObject: Pumped dual-porosity fractured aquifer with matrix blocks and an overlying aquitard
visual.geometryNotes: Maintain the radial well and fracture-matrix cross-section throughout. Use early, intermediate, and late time bands on the drawdown axis and preserve the omega slider as the same object that changes the exchange-arrow thickness.
